decomposition.title: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing and enterprise solutions; kitchen and bath products, wellness, hospitality, and digital innovation
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred future hosting opportunity approximately $15M-$75M over 3-5 years, based on enterprise-scale data center exit scope including SAP ecosystem, VMware/VxRail estate, physical and virtual Linux/Windows servers, databases, backup/DR, identity, network connectivity, monitoring, logging, and security tooling. Phase 0 advisory/evaluation portion alone is likely materially smaller but not separately priced in the document.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. Inferred likely 3-5 years for a future enterprise hosting/DCaaS/cloud/hybrid hosting commercial model; this RFP itself is a Phase 0 evaluation and business-case exercise with subsequent phases after vendor selection.

requirements.text: Vendors must submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities.
Vendors must submit proposals detailing high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing.
Vendors must submit proposals detailing operating model and support framework.
Vendors must submit proposals detailing commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities under a shared responsibility framework.
Vendors are expected to propose a governance, security, and compliance approach.
Vendors are expected to propose an operations and support model.
Proposed models should demonstrate how they can support a progressive transition away from the current Data Center footprint while maintaining business continuity and operational stability.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate a hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is a common source of hidden compliance gating, but the document does not specify a prohibited jurisdiction or mandatory location.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: A region strategy and data residency approach is required, but no specific country, state, or sovereignty rule is stated.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable and clearly state assumptions.
In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
In-scope services include commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Current core hosting environment is Wisconsin-based.

requirements.text: In-scope services include Windows and Linux virtual machines.
In-scope services include databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and other supported platforms.
In-scope services include middleware and integration platforms.
In-scope services include file storage systems and shared infrastructure services.
In-scope services include backup and disaster recovery solutions.
In-scope services include identity and access management integrations.
In-scope services include network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments.
In-scope services include monitoring, logging, and security tooling.
In-scope services include selected OT-adjacent workloads, limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent scope is constrained to read-only or controlled integration scenarios; proposing broader OT intervention would be outside stated scope.

requirements.text: Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning, and execution activities, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Scope limitation is easy to miss because it is framed as out-of-scope but directly affects pricing and delivery assumptions.

requirements.text: Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities such as code changes, re-platforming, and re-architecting are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Scope limitation is easy to miss and prevents assuming app modernization/refactoring services are part of this RFP.

requirements.text: Detailed low-level design and configuration-level architecture beyond high-level solution design and reference architecture are out of scope.
Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Managed services delivery commitments are out of scope even though pricing guidelines request consulting, support, or managed services costs where applicable.

requirements.text: Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Vendors only need to demonstrate capability and readiness; committing to execute audits or certifications would exceed the stated scope.

requirements.text: Tool implementation, automation deployment, or integration development is out of scope unless required to illustrate proposed solution capability.
End-user training, change management execution, and organizational transformation activities are out of scope.
Data cleansing, data transformation, or archival activities beyond indicative approaches are out of scope.
Detailed testing execution and validation activities, including UAT, SIT, or performance testing, are out of scope; only approach and methodology are expected.
Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and evidence of certifications is required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The certification evidence requirement is mandatory but the specific certifications are not listed in the main document.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications not named.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This requirement applies specifically to DCaaS providers and points to the Vendor Response Template for details not included in the provided document.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers; specific attestations not named in provided document.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Use of the external inventory baseline is mandatory for sizing and pricing, but the spreadsheet is outside the provided document.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory baseline is required for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing; failure to use it could make pricing non-comparable.

requirements.text: Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission must be via the designated communication channel by the defined timeline; no explicit late-bid rejection language is stated, but this is a mandatory instruction.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Use of the provided response template is mandatory and is a common formatting compliance trap.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The document states simple Yes/No answers will not be considered sufficient, making insufficient template completion a serious response-compliance risk.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification questions must use the provided Q&A template and be sent within timeline; missing the deadline may prevent clarifications.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: All communications are restricted to the named email channel, including copy to a distribution mailbox.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured, and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of the RFP and the inventory baseline provided.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including foundational setup costs such as landing zone, security, governance, and compliance configurations.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including application and infrastructure hosting costs for compute, storage, networking, and platform services.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including migration / implementation costs if applicable, including transition or onboarding activities.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Separate SAP and non-SAP cost views are mandatory and easy to miss in pricing workbooks.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: USD-only pricing is mandatory and can cause commercial non-compliance for global bidders if another currency is used.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Commercial values must be in United States Dollars.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler expects pricing mechanisms tied to actual paid prices rather than generic list-price discounts.

requirements.text: Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, making this a major commercial risk even though it is not phrased as automatic rejection.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the proposed solution.
The proposed governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model.
The proposed governance overview should include reporting and review mechanisms such as periodic status updates and performance tracking.
The proposed governance overview should include an escalation framework for issue resolution.
The proposed governance overview should include key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
All information shared as part of this RFP is confidential and must be treated accordingly by participating vendors.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality terms are in the appendix and can be missed by solution/pricing teams handling RFP materials.

requirements.text: Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Use restriction is in the appendix and limits internal and partner use of Kohler-provided information.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Third-party disclosure restriction is buried in the appendix and may affect subcontractors, cloud partners, offshore teams, and external advisors.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Broad compliance obligation appears in the appendix, but exact standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations are not specified.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Relevant obligations are described generally for enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.

ambiguities: No clause in the provided document explicitly states that a non-compliant proposal will be automatically rejected or disqualified before evaluation; therefore eliminatory_count is set to 0 and hard_disqualifiers is empty.

The Evaluation Template (Excel) is repeatedly referenced as containing detailed requirements, scoring, weightages, and DCaaS compliance-attestation details, but it was not included in the provided document. Mandatory requirements may exist there that cannot be extracted from this text.
The Inventory Baseline spreadsheet is referenced as comprehensive as of 29 May 2026 and as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and pricing, but it was not included in the provided document.
Contract duration is not explicitly stated. Duration is inferred only from the enterprise hosting/DC-exit nature of the scope and subsequent-phase language.

Estimated deal size or budget is not explicitly stated. Deal size is inferred from enterprise-scale hosting of a Wisconsin data center estate including SAP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, databases, identity, network, backup/DR, monitoring, and security tooling.

Required certifications are not named. The document only states that evidence of certifications is required and that DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.

DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards, with more information stated to be in the Vendor Response Template. [PAGE 7]

Vendors must propose a region strategy and data residency approach. This is a potential gating compliance topic, although no explicit data-residency prohibition or jurisdiction is stated in the provided text. [PAGE 6]
All information shared as part of the RFP is confidential and must be used solely for responding to the RFP. [PAGE 11]

Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent. This could affect subcontractor, cloud partner, or offshore solutioning workflows. [PAGE 11]
All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars. [PAGE 9]
Payment terms shorter than net 90 will not be considered competitive and proposals should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms. [PAGE 10]

similar_deals.title: Kohler Project Polaris — Global Manufacturing Hybrid Hosting / Data-Center Exit
similar_deals.deal_size_usd: Phase 0 benchmarks found at $279,533.42 and $595,000; downstream 36-month scenario found at $30,667,392; broader future opportunity inferred at $15M-$50M+ over 3-5 years
similar_deals.outcome: Outcome not provided in intelligence; do not treat as a proven win
similar_deals.why_relevant: Directly matches the RFP title, client, geography, scope, SAP ecosystem, VMware/VxRail estate, physical and virtual Linux/Windows servers, databases, backup/DR, IAM, network, monitoring, logging, security tooling, target architecture, operating model, transition, and commercial transparency requirements.
similar_deals.key_lesson: The strongest reusable pattern is a 12-week vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab that compares cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration workload by workload, with Kohler-owned outputs.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout
similar_deals.why_relevant: Strong fit on manufacturing, SAP criticality, global footprint, 4-year duration, and $15M-$75M scale. Relevant to Kohler because SAP-aware hosting and transition risk will likely be decisive.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won because of deep SAP S/4HANA manufacturing capability, largest SAP RISE team in the vertical, zero-downtime methodology, global follow-the-sun delivery, and decisive executive sponsor alignment.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Direct manufacturing IT transformation analogue. Supports positioning around manufacturing domain depth, rapid mobilization, and trust, though geography was Germany rather than U.S. and the scope is not explicitly DCaaS/hybrid hosting.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won on fastest mobilization, team readiness within 2 weeks, domain credentials, and client trust.

similar_deals.title: Supply Chain AI Platform
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant for SAP adjacency, enterprise integration complexity, U.S./multi-country footprint, and the need to show accelerators that reduce transition risk and timeline.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Proprietary accelerators and pre-built SAP plus Oracle connectors won by reducing the delivery timeline by 8 months; demonstrable IP beat lower hourly rates.

similar_deals.title: Telecom Network Automation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Useful U.S. infrastructure analogue for network operations, automation, security certification, and reference-led buying behavior. Supports the need for U.S. peer references in the Kohler response.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won through domain-specific automation expertise, ISO 27001, AWS Premier Partner credentials, and strong U.S. peer references.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Direct cautionary manufacturing comparable in the same size/duration band. Highly relevant to Kohler because U.S./Wisconsin/global hosting pursuits often hide legal entity, data residency, security, subcontractor, and transition obligations in appendices.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because the team missed a local entity requirement in the compliance appendix; compliance misses can outweigh technical merit.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Another direct manufacturing loss showing the same failure mode. Supports assigning a dedicated compliance owner before submission.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost due to missed local entity requirement, reinforcing that compliance discipline is mandatory in manufacturing transformation pursuits.

similar_deals.title: Healthcare IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Not manufacturing, but relevant to managed IT pricing discipline. Kohler requires commercial transparency, so an expensive architecture without modular options and clear value bridge is a major risk.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because pricing was 20% above the winning bid despite a better solution.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 42%. This is a credible but not favored position. Positive evidence: the past-proposal knowledge base contains a near-exact Kohler Project Polaris match with directly reusable architecture, transition, operating model, governance, security, TCO, and 12-week Hosting Decision Lab content; deal corpus includes relevant wins such as DEAL-003 manufacturing/SAP global rollout at $71M over 4 years, DEAL-089 manufacturing IT transformation at $22M, and enterprise infrastructure analogues showing that SAP credibility, accelerators, global delivery, and peer references can win. Negative evidence: no public procurement data is available, so competitor incumbency and awarded price benchmarks cannot be validated; the Kohler-specific proposal intelligence has no stated outcome and cannot be claimed as a prior win; closest proven wins are adjacent rather than exact U.S. manufacturing DCaaS/hybrid-hosting evaluation wins; comparable manufacturing losses DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 were lost on compliance misses; and pricing losses DEAL-054 and DEAL-029 show that a technically superior but overpriced solution can lose. Because only two substantive intelligence layers are available and procurement ground truth is absent, confidence is medium-low and the probability is deliberately conservative.
capability_gaps: No evidenced Kohler incumbent relationship or executive sponsor alignment in the provided intelligence, which is a gap against an enterprise hosting decision where operating model, transition accountability, and trust are central evaluation factors.

No public procurement award data is available to validate likely competitors, winning price bands, or incumbent advantage; this creates a pricing and competitive-positioning gap against the RFP requirement for commercial and pricing structure.

The intelligence does not prove a directly comparable U.S. manufacturing data-center-exit / DCaaS / hybrid-hosting win at Kohler scale; closest wins are SAP transformation, manufacturing IT transformation, and enterprise platform migration, not an exact hosting-options advisory-to-run sequence.

Need stronger proof for VMware/VxRail, physical server, backup/DR, IAM, logging, monitoring, and security-tooling transition at enterprise scale; these are explicit scope elements in the RFP but the strongest deal proof is SAP/manufacturing-led rather than full hosting-stack-led.

Compliance appendix risk remains unresolved: prior manufacturing losses were caused by missed local-entity requirements, so U.S./Wisconsin contracting entity, data residency, cross-border support, subcontractor, insurance, audit, security-certification, transition-out, and DR-location requirements must be verified before bid submission.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral 12-week Hosting Decision Lab: position Polaris as a Kohler-owned decision factory, not a forced cloud migration. Deliver workload placement scorecards, target landing-zone blueprints, open-book USD TCO, migration waves, rollback gates, shared-responsibility RACI, DR/security/observability designs, and executive decision package.

Manufacturing-grade SAP-aware hosting transition: make SAP, manufacturing continuity, zero-downtime methods, batch/dependency mapping, HANA/Basis/RISE/private-cloud considerations, backup/DR, and follow-the-sun support central to the solution rather than treating SAP as just another workload.

Risk-controlled hybrid data-center exit: show how Kohler can progressively reduce the Wisconsin data-center footprint while maintaining continuity for plants, hospitality, analytics, connected products, and customer-facing platforms through phased migration, coexistence governance, rollback gates, and hypercare.

Commercial transparency and optionality: provide clear Phase 0 fixed fee, open-book TCO, one-time versus recurring costs, unit assumptions, licensing impacts, migration-wave pricing, FinOps guardrails, and separable downstream options for DCaaS, hybrid managed services, and cloud transformation.

Proof through accelerators and references: use data-center-exit tooling, SAP dependency-mapping accelerators, VMware/VxRail discovery templates, CMDB normalization, TCO models, migration-factory playbooks, and near-peer manufacturing references to reduce perceived delivery risk.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so competitor win patterns and awarded price benchmarks cannot be validated. Confidence is therefore medium-low rather than high.

The strongest internal RFP/proposal match is Kohler Project Polaris itself, but the provided intelligence does not state an outcome; it should be treated as reusable proposal intelligence, not proof of a prior win.

Relevant wins exist in manufacturing/SAP and enterprise transformation, but there is no explicit exact-match win for a U.S. manufacturing hybrid-hosting/DCaaS evaluation that converted into a $15M-$75M run contract.
Manufacturing losses DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 show that a small missed local-entity or compliance requirement can override a strong technical solution.
Pricing risk is material because losses DEAL-054 and DEAL-029 show that being approximately 20% above the winning bid can lose even with a superior solution.
Kohler may value vendor neutrality in Phase 0; an overly cloud-forward or platform-biased answer could weaken credibility.
Future downstream scope is inferred, not stated. Over-positioning the $15M-$75M managed hosting opportunity in the Phase 0 response could appear presumptive unless tied to optional future scenarios.

RFP requires detailed target architecture, transition sequencing, operating model, security/governance, landing zones, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, region strategy, and data residency; a generic advisory response would be noncompetitive.
must_fix_before_bidding: Create a full compliance matrix across every RFP schedule and appendix, with named ownership for U.S./Wisconsin legal entity, data residency, security certifications, insurance, subcontractors, DR, audit rights, background checks, transition-in, and transition-out obligations.

Lock a Phase 0 fixed-fee price and pricing strategy. Use the known internal benchmarks of $279,533.42 and $595,000 for the 12-week Hosting Decision Lab, and avoid an over-engineered price position that could be 20% above market.
Secure 2-3 near-peer references before submission: ideally U.S. manufacturing, global manufacturing with SAP, and enterprise data-center-exit / hybrid hosting / VMware modernization.

Name credible solution leads for SAP, hybrid cloud/DCaaS, VMware/VxRail, network connectivity, security/governance, DR/backup, and operating model; the proposal should not appear to be generic cloud advisory.

Include three commercial/architecture options: low-risk stabilize-and-host, balanced hybrid modernization, and cloud-forward transformation, each with workload-placement logic, TCO assumptions, migration sequencing, and risk tradeoffs.

Build an explicit Kohler-specific workload assessment and dependency-mapping approach covering SAP, VMware/VxRail, physical and virtual Linux/Windows servers, databases, IAM, backup/DR, logging, monitoring, security tooling, manufacturing integrations, hospitality operations, and connected/digital products.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium (confidence: medium) — TCS is a plausible challenger because web intelligence shows strong cloud modernization, AI-led ITOps and manufacturing capabilities, plus a third-party LinkedIn signal for a TCS Software Developer in Waukesha and 1,000+ U.S. TCS jobs. However, high likelihood is not justified because there is no public TCS-Kohler win, no public procurement award, and no confirmed Wisconsin hiring specifically for SAP Basis, VMware, DCaaS, hybrid cloud or hosting operations.
competitors.predicted_positioning: TCS will likely position as an AI-led enterprise transformation and cloud modernization partner for manufacturing, emphasizing Q1 FY27 TCV of $9.5B, annualized AI revenue of $2.6B, the $800M SKF mega deal, ServiceNow partnership, AI-led ITOps, enterprise transformation, SAP/cloud modernization, digital manufacturing, CPG capabilities and global delivery. Source basis: TCS Q1 FY27 and Q4 FY26 web/financial releases; TCS manufacturing, alliances and CPG capability pages. They will likely attack the incumbent by offering a cost-takeout and multi-cloud/SAP modernization lane rather than claiming account incumbency.
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Full downstream hosting / migration / managed-services bid likely $18M-$45M over 3-5 years; Phase 0 advisory likely $250K-$750K. Calculation/evidence: no public procurement awards are available, so price is anchored to the RFP-inferred $15M-$75M opportunity and TCS Q1 FY27 operating margin of 24.0%. Margin floor math: minimum sustainable revenue = delivery cost / (1 - 0.24) = 1.316x delivery cost. Revenue/employee cannot be calculated from the supplied evidence because no TCS employee count was provided; financial basis is therefore margin-only. Confidence: medium-low because no public Kohler/TCS contract value exists.
their_strengths: Strong financial capacity: Q1 FY27 revenue of $7.624B, operating margin of 24.0%, net margin of 19.2%, Q1 TCV of $9.5B and annualized AI revenue of $2.6B from TCS web/financial intelligence.
Large-deal proof: Q1 FY27 included an $800M SKF mega deal and multi-million-dollar ServiceNow and Fortune Global 50 deals, cited in TCS web intelligence.

Relevant manufacturing and enterprise capabilities proven by public capability pages: TCS InTwin, Digital Manufacturing Platform, Crystallus, Siemens/PTC/Dassault/AVEVA/ABB alliances, Boomi integration and CPG/distribution offerings.
U.S. hiring breadth: LinkedIn-indexed results showed 1,000+ U.S. TCS jobs and a Waukesha, Wisconsin Software Developer posting, per job posting intelligence.
their_weaknesses: No recent contract wins in Wisconsin or with Kohler were identified for TCS in the supplied evidence.

Public account signal favors Cognizant: web intelligence cites the January 29, 2026 Cognizant-Kohler extension for cloud management, infrastructure modernization, AI-driven security and observability, not a TCS award.
Wisconsin job signal is weak for this RFP: the cited Waukesha role is Software Developer, not confirmed SAP Basis, VMware, hybrid hosting, DCaaS, security operations or data-center-exit staffing.
High 24.0% operating margin creates a higher pricing floor: cost / (1 - 0.24) = 1.316x, making aggressive low-price bidding less credible unless TCS narrows scope or uses offshore leverage.

solution_options.name: Premium Hybrid Exit and Resilience Transformation
solution_options.description: Premium lowest-risk enterprise hybrid hosting and data-center-exit program. HCLTech leads a provider-neutral Phase 0, then assumes fuller accountability for landing zone build, migration factory execution, hybrid/DCaaS transition, SAP and VMware/VxRail disposition support, enhanced DR, security controls, observability, FinOps and 24x7 managed operations across a 48-month term.

Azure-first multi-subscription landing zone with hybrid connectivity, identity integration and environment segregation
Enterprise migration factory for SAP-adjacent, VMware/VxRail, physical Linux/Windows, database, backup/DR and security-tooling workloads
Enhanced 24x7 managed operations with approximately 48 average FTE coverage across SDM, architects, SRE, DBA, network, security, FinOps and ITSM roles
Premium resilience model including DR test blueprint, rollback gates, parallel run, security-by-design controls and observability/AIOps integration

pricing.pricing_structure: Recommended Option B fixed-plus-managed-services model: Phase 0 fixed fee is included inside transformation cost. Full 36-month program math: People $13.700M + Infrastructure $7.000M + Migration/Transformation $3.750M + Governance/Tooling $1.850M = $26.300M subtotal. Contingency at 12% = $3.156M. Total cost before margin = $29.456M. HCLTech margin at 17.5% of revenue = $6.248M. Total client price = $35.704M. Cloud/security unit cost used from live Azure pricing: Defender for Cloud $15/node-month x 220 enterprise nodes x 36 months = $118,800 included in the infrastructure layer; remaining cloud/DCaaS/colo/software charges should be contracted as transparent pass-through or capped consumption bands after Phase 0 sizing.
